mission.title: envoy: Support for X-Forwarded-Host header
mission.description: Support for X-Forwarded-Host header. Requested by 13+ users.

steps.title: Check current envoy deployment
steps.description:
Verify your envoy version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n envoy -l app.kubernetes.io/name=envoy
helm list -n envoy 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working envoy installation.

steps.title: Review envoy configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant envoy configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n envoy -l app.kubernetes.io/name=envoy
kubectl get configmap -n envoy -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=envoy
```
*Title*: *Support for X-Forwarded-Host header*

*Description*:
Today envoy does not seem to forward the original host in a X-Forwarded-Host header, it just rewrites the original one and apparently there is no way to get the original host back again.

steps.title: Apply the fix for Support for X-Forwarded-Host header
steps.description:
SGTM. I'm not sure if we want to config guard it or not. On one hand, I think it would be OK to not config guard if we only set when a host mutation happens as you point out. On the other hand, it's possible that upstreams are not expecting us to set it and we might break them. /maintainers any
```yaml
+----------+
 |          |
 |   App    |
 |          |
 +----------+
      ^ Host: app.namespace.svc.cluster.local
      | X-Forwarded-Host: example.com
 +----------+
 |          |
 |  Envoy   |
 |          |
 +----------+
      ^
      | Host:example.com
      |
      |
     User
```

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n envoy -l app.kubernetes.io/name=envoy
kubectl get events -n envoy --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "Support for X-Forwarded-Host header" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary: SGTM. I'm not sure if we want to config guard it or not. On one hand, I think it would be OK to not config guard if we only set when a host mutation happens as you point out. On the other hand, it's possible that upstreams are not expecting us to set it and we might break them. /maintainers any thoughts?
codeSnippets:
+----------+
 |          |
 |   App    |
 |          |
 +----------+
      ^ Host: app.namespace.svc.cluster.local
      | X-Forwarded-Host: example.com
 +----------+
 |          |
 |  Envoy   |
 |          |
 +----------+
      ^
      | Host:example.com
      |
      |
     User

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with envoy installed or the issue environment reproducible.
